title: SELECT Statement — Query data
summary: Complete SELECT statement: predicates (WHERE), operators (LIKE, BETWEEN, IN, IS NULL), aliases, concatenation, aggregate functions (COUNT, MAX, MIN, SUM, AVG), GROUP BY, and JOIN types (INNER, OUTER, NATURAL, multi-table).
content_html:
SELECT Statement — Query data

SELECT is a command to read data from the database. Full syntax:

SELECT column1, column2, ...
FROM table_name
WHERE condition
GROUP BY column
HAVING group_condition
ORDER BY column ASC|DESC;

1. Restriction — WHERE clause (Predicate)

Restriction is line filtering — only taking lines that meet the condition. WHERE clause used predicate (predicate) to determine conditions.

SELECT * FROM Staff
WHERE BranchID = 101;

Important predicate operators

LIKE — Matches string pattern

  • % = represents any zero or more characters
  • _ = represents exactly any 1 character
SELECT * FROM Staff WHERE StaffName LIKE 'A%';
SELECT * FROM Staff WHERE StaffName LIKE '%son';
SELECT * FROM Staff WHERE StaffName LIKE '_oe%';
SELECT * FROM Staff WHERE StaffName LIKE '%an%';

Explanation: 'A%' = name starts with A. '%son' = ends with son. '_oe%' = 2nd and 3rd characters are 'oe'. '%an%' = contains 'an' in any position.

BETWEEN — Value range (inclusive)

SELECT * FROM Inspection
WHERE InspDate BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-03-31';

SELECT * FROM Product
WHERE Price BETWEEN 100 AND 500;

BETWEEN includes on both ends.

IN and NOT IN — Check the list

SELECT * FROM Staff
WHERE BranchID IN (101, 102, 105);

SELECT * FROM Staff
WHERE BranchID NOT IN (103, 104);

IS NULL and IS NOT NULL

SELECT * FROM Staff WHERE PhoneNumber IS NULL;
SELECT * FROM Staff WHERE Email IS NOT NULL;

NO write WHERE PhoneNumber = NULL — always use IS NULL because NULL cannot be compared using the = operator.

AND and OR — Combine conditions

SELECT * FROM Staff
WHERE BranchID = 101 AND Gender = 'F';

SELECT * FROM Staff
WHERE BranchID = 101 OR BranchID = 102;

SELECT * FROM Staff
WHERE Gender = 'F'
  AND (BranchID = 101 OR BranchID = 102);

AND has higher precedence than OR — use parentheses for clarity.

2. Projection — Select a specific column

Projection is to select only the necessary columns (instead of SELECT *):

SELECT StaffID, StaffName, BranchID
FROM Staff;

Aliases — Set the alias name

SELECT StaffName AS 'Employee Name',
       BranchID  AS 'Branch'
FROM Staff;

SELECT s.StaffID, s.StaffName, b.BranchName
FROM Staff s
JOIN Branch b ON s.BranchID = b.BranchID;

Alias for table (s, b) help make queries more concise when JOIN multiple tables.

Concatenation — String concatenation

SELECT CONCAT(FirstName, ' ', LastName) AS 'Full Name'
FROM Person;

SELECT FirstName || ' ' || LastName AS 'Full Name'
FROM Person;

MySQL used CONCAT(). SQL standard used || (Oracle, PostgreSQL).

3. Aggregate Functions — Aggregate functions

HamDescriptionFor example
COUNT(*)Count all lines (including NULL)SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Staff;
COUNT(col)Count rows with non-NULL values in the columnSELECT COUNT(Email) FROM Staff;
MAX(col)Greatest valueSELECT MAX(Score) FROM Result;
MIN(col)Minimum valueSELECT MIN(Price) FROM Product;
SUM(col)TotalSELECT SUM(Amount) FROM Order;
AVG(col)AverageSELECT AVG(Mark) FROM Enrolment;

GROUP BY — Group by column

GROUP BY combined with aggregate functions to calculate by group:

SELECT BranchID, COUNT(*) AS StaffCount
FROM Staff
GROUP BY BranchID;

Results: Number of employees by each branch.

SELECT BranchID, Gender, COUNT(*) AS Count
FROM Staff
GROUP BY BranchID, Gender;

SELECT SubjectID,
       AVG(Mark)    AS AvgMark,
       MAX(Mark)    AS TopMark,
       MIN(Mark)    AS LowestMark,
       COUNT(*)     AS StudentCount
FROM Enrolment
WHERE Mark IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY SubjectID
HAVING AVG(Mark) > 60
ORDER BY AvgMark DESC;

HAVING Filter the results after GROUP BY (like WHERE but for groups). The example above only shows subjects with an average score above 60.

4. JOINs — Join tables

History: Old JOIN style (pre-SQL-92)

SELECT s.StudentName, e.SubjectID, e.Mark
FROM Student s, Enrolment e
WHERE s.StudentID = e.StudentID;

This is the old syntax — it still works, but it's not recommended because it's confusing when there are multiple tables.

INNER JOIN — Get only matching rows in both tables

SELECT s.StudentName, e.SubjectID, e.Mark
FROM Student s
INNER JOIN Enrolment e ON s.StudentID = e.StudentID;

Example from lecture: Anna has no enrolment → INNER JOIN will remove Anna is cured.

StudentNameSubjectIDMark
JohnCOS10175
JohnCOS10282
MaryCOS10188
Anna(not appearing — no enrolment)

LEFT OUTER JOIN / RIGHT OUTER JOIN — Holds all rows from a table

SELECT s.StudentName, e.SubjectID, e.Mark
FROM Enrolment e
RIGHT JOIN Student s ON e.StudentID = s.StudentID;

RIGHT JOIN holds all rows from the right table (Student). Anna appears with SubjectID = NULL and Mark = NULL:

StudentNameSubjectIDMark
JohnCOS10175
JohnCOS10282
MaryCOS10188
AnnaNULLNULL

NATURAL JOIN — Automatically matches columns of the same name

SELECT StudentName, SubjectID, Mark
FROM Student
NATURAL JOIN Enrolment;

NATURAL JOIN automatically matches columns with the same name in both tables. Rarely used because it can easily cause errors if a new column is added to the table.

Multi-table JOIN — JOIN multiple tables

SELECT s.StaffName, p.PropertyAddress, i.InspDate, i.Comments
FROM Staff s
INNER JOIN PropertyInspection i ON s.StaffID = i.StaffID
INNER JOIN Property p ON i.PropertyID = p.PropertyID
WHERE i.InspDate BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-06-30'
ORDER BY i.InspDate;

JOIN three tables: Staff → PropertyInspection → Property. Each JOIN adds a table to the result by matching FKs.

Query Execution Strategy

RDBMS does not execute queries in the order you write them. It optimizes:

  1. Parse the WHERE clause to filter as soon as possible
  2. Chọn bảng có ít dòng nhất để bắt đầu
  3. Use index if available
  4. JOIN in optimal order

Use EXPLAIN SELECT ... to see MySQL's execution plan.
